content:
First, label the trapezoid so that 
 AD‖BC with AD = a<BC = b, 
 the two non‐parallel sides (the “legs”) each have length ℓ, 
and the height is h.  
By symmetry of an isosceles trapezoid we may place  
 A=(−a/2,0), D=(a/2,0), B=(−b/2,h), C=(b/2,h).  

(1)  From the perimeter data  
 Per(ABCD)=2ℓ+a+b=120,  
 Per(ABC)=ℓ+b+AC=114.  

(2)  By symmetry ΔABC≅ΔDBC, so AC=DB.  Call that common third side s.  Then  
 ℓ+b+s=114,  
 s=114−ℓ−b.  

(3)  In ΔDBC the incenter has x–coordinate  
 x_I = [BC·x_D +CD·x_B +DB·x_C]/(BC+CD+DB)  
      = [b·(a/2) + ℓ·(−b/2) + s·(b/2)]/(ℓ+b+s)  
      = b·(a−ℓ+s)/(2·114).  
By symmetry the two incenters are at x=±x_I so their distance is 2x_I.  We are told 2x_I=16, hence  
 b(a−ℓ+s)=16·114/2=912.  

(4)  Meanwhile the legs give  
 ℓ² = h² +((b−a)/2)²,  
and  
 s² = h² +((b+a)/2)².  
Subtraction shows  
 s² −ℓ² = ab,  
so s=√(ℓ²+ab).  But also s=114−ℓ−b.  

(5)  One now has a small system in (a,b,ℓ):  
 2ℓ +a +b =120,  
 ℓ +b +√(ℓ²+ab)=114,  
 b(a−ℓ+114−ℓ−b)=912.  
A quick algebraic elimination (or direct guessing) yields  
 a=22, b=48, ℓ=25,  
and then  
 h = √(ℓ²−((b−a)/2)²) = √(625−(26²/4)) = √456 =2√114.  

Finally the area of the trapezoid is  
 Area = (a+b)/2·h = (22+48)/2 ·2√114 =70√114.  
Hence m=70, n=114, and 100m+n=7000+114=7114.  

Answer: \boxed{7114}.

and the height is h.  
By symmetry of an isosceles trapezoid we may place

 Per(ABC)=ℓ+b+AC=114.  

(2)  By symmetry ΔABC≅ΔDBC, so AC=DB.  Call that common third side s.  Then

(3)  In ΔDBC the incenter has x–coordinate  
 x_I = [BC·x_D +CD·x_B +DB·x_C]/(BC+CD+DB)
